name: Aim
tagline: Self-hosted, open-source experiment tracker built for 10,000s of training runs
metaDescription: Aim is a self-hosted, open-source experiment tracker that logs ML training runs and compares them in a fast web UI, with a Python SDK to query your metadata.
overview: Aim is an open-source, self-hosted tool for tracking machine learning experiments. You log training runs and any related metadata from your code, then explore and compare them in a web UI. It is designed to handle tens of thousands of runs without slowing down.

It fits the experiment-tracking corner of MLOps: the place where you keep a record of every training run, its hyperparameters, and its metrics so you can see what worked. Because Aim runs on your own machine or server, you keep full control of your data instead of sending it to a hosted service.

It is aimed at ML engineers and researchers who run many experiments and want one dashboard to group, filter, and compare them. Alongside the UI, a Python SDK lets you query tracked metadata programmatically, which is handy for automation and analysis inside Jupyter notebooks.
features: Logs training runs and arbitrary AI metadata, with integrations for popular ML frameworks
Web UI to visualize, group, aggregate, and compare runs across experiments
Query runs using Python expressions, both in the UI and through the SDK
Tracks system info and resource usage during training
Organize runs with tags and experiments plus a centralized dashboard
Self-hosted, so your experiment data stays on your own infrastructure

gettingStarted.intro: Install Aim with pip, add a few lines to your training script to track metrics, then launch the UI to compare runs.

steps.heading: Track a run in your code
steps.body: Create a Run, log your hyperparameters, and track metrics in your training loop.
steps.code:
from aim import Run

# Initialize a new run
run = Run()

# Log run parameters
run["hparams"] = {
    "learning_rate": 0.001,
    "batch_size": 32,
}

# Log metrics
for i in range(10):
    run.track(i, name='loss', step=i, context={ "subset":"train" })
    run.track(i, name='acc', step=i, context={ "subset":"train" })

steps.heading: Open the UI
steps.body: Start the Aim web interface to explore and compare your tracked runs.

useCases: Compare hyperparameter sweeps across many runs to find the configuration that trains best
Keep a self-hosted record of all experiments on your own server instead of a third-party service
Query tracked metrics from a Jupyter notebook for custom analysis or reporting
Monitor metrics and system resource usage while a training job is running
